title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
TipDownload Notebook





Download as Jupyter Notebook

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Understand the difference between expected value and risk-aware optimization
Apply risk measures like AVaR (Conditional Value at Risk)
Formulate and solve risk-constrained optimization problems
Choose appropriate risk levels for different applications

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Prerequisites
Complete Monte Carlo Basics before this tutorial.

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Why Consider Risk?
Standard optimization minimizes expected cost:
\[
\min_{\vc{x}} \E_{\params}[f(\vc{x}, \params)]
\]
But expected value ignores tail risk: rare but catastrophic outcomes.
Example: Portfolio optimization

Strategy A: Expected return = 10%, occasionally loses 50%
Strategy B: Expected return = 8%, never loses more than 15%

A risk-averse investor might prefer Strategy B despite lower expected return.

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Risk Measures
A risk measure \(\rho\) maps a random outcome to a single number representing “risk.”

Mean (Risk-Neutral)
\[
\rho_{mean}[X] = \E_X[X]
\]
Treats all outcomes equally regardless of magnitude.


Mean + Standard Deviation
\[
\rho_{\alpha}[X] = \E_X[X] + \alpha \cdot \sqrt{\Var_X[X]}
\]
Penalizes variability. Higher \(\alpha\) = more risk-averse.


Value at Risk (VaR)
\[
\text{VaR}_\beta[X] = \inf\{t : \Pr(X \leq t) \geq \beta\}
\]
The \(\beta\)-quantile of the distribution. VaR\(_{0.95}\) is the 95th percentile.
Problem: VaR ignores outcomes beyond the threshold.


Average Value at Risk (AVaR / CVaR)
\[
\text{AVaR}_\beta[X] = \E_X[X \,|\, X > \text{VaR}_\beta[X]]
\]
The expected value given that we’re in the worst \((1-\beta)\) tail.
Properties:

Coherent risk measure (satisfies desirable axioms)
\(\text{AVaR}_0 = \E_X[X]\) (mean)
\(\text{AVaR}_1 \to \max(X)\) (worst case)

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Visualizing Risk Measures

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.probability.risk.gaussian import GaussianAnalyticalRiskMeasures
from pyapprox.typing.probability.univariate.gaussian import GaussianMarginal

bkd = NumpyBkd()
np.random.seed(42)


# Gaussian distribution using pyapprox.typing
mu, sigma = 0, 1
risk_measures = GaussianAnalyticalRiskMeasures(mu, sigma)
gauss = GaussianMarginal(mean=mu, stdev=sigma, bkd=bkd)

# Compare risk measures
beta_values = np.linspace(0.01, 0.99, 50)
avar_values = [risk_measures.AVaR(beta) for beta in beta_values]
# VaR = inverse CDF (quantile function)
var_values = bkd.to_numpy(gauss.invcdf(bkd.asarray([beta_values]))).flatten()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

# PDF with risk levels
ax = axes[0]
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 200)
x_2d = bkd.asarray([x])  # Shape (1, 200) for PyApprox convention
pdf = bkd.to_numpy(gauss(x_2d)).flatten()
var_95 = bkd.to_numpy(gauss.invcdf(bkd.asarray([[0.95]])))[0, 0]

ax.plot(x, pdf, 'b-', linewidth=2, label='PDF')
ax.axvline(risk_measures.mean(), color='g', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean = {mu:.1f}')
ax.axvline(var_95, color='r', linestyle='--', label=f'VaR$_{{0.95}}$ = {var_95:.2f}')
ax.axvline(risk_measures.AVaR(0.95), color='orange', linestyle='--', label=f'AVaR$_{{0.95}}$ = {risk_measures.AVaR(0.95):.2f}')
ax.fill_between(x[x > var_95], pdf[x > var_95], alpha=0.3, color='red', label='5% tail')
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Density')
ax.set_title('Risk Measures on Standard Normal')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# AVaR vs beta
ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(beta_values, avar_values, 'b-', linewidth=2, label='AVaR')
ax.plot(beta_values, var_values, 'r--', linewidth=2, label='VaR')
ax.axhline(mu, color='g', linestyle='--', label='Mean')
ax.set_xlabel(r'Risk level $\beta$')
ax.set_ylabel('Risk measure value')
ax.set_title(r'AVaR and VaR vs $\beta$')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
AVaR Optimization

Problem Formulation
Minimize the Average Value at Risk:
\[
\min_{\vc{x}} \text{AVaR}_\beta[f(\vc{x}, \params)]
\]


Reformulation as Constrained Optimization
AVaR can be computed as:
\[
\text{AVaR}_\beta[f] = \min_{t} \left\{ t + \frac{1}{1-\beta} \E_{\params}[(f - t)^+] \right\}
\]
where \((f - t)^+ = \max(0, f - t)\).
For discrete scenarios \(\{f_1, ..., f_n\}\):
\[
\text{AVaR}_\beta = \min_{t, s_i} \left\{ t + \frac{1}{n(1-\beta)} \sum_{i=1}^n s_i \right\}
\]
subject to \(s_i \geq f_i - t\) and \(s_i \geq 0\).

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Example: Portfolio Optimization
Consider choosing allocation between risky and safe assets.

# Simple portfolio model
def portfolio_return(allocation, scenarios):
    """
    Portfolio return given allocation to risky asset.

    Parameters
    ----------
    allocation : float in [0, 1]
        Fraction allocated to risky asset.
    scenarios : array
        Random market returns for risky asset.

    Returns
    -------
    array
        Portfolio returns for each scenario.
    """
    risky_return = scenarios
    safe_return = 0.03  # 3% risk-free rate
    return allocation * risky_return + (1 - allocation) * safe_return


# Generate market scenarios (asymmetric - fat left tail)
n_scenarios = 1000
market_mean = 0.10  # 10% expected return
market_vol = 0.20   # 20% volatility

# Use mixture for fat tail
normal_returns = np.random.normal(market_mean, market_vol, int(0.9 * n_scenarios))
crash_returns = np.random.normal(-0.30, 0.10, int(0.1 * n_scenarios))  # Rare crashes
market_scenarios = np.concatenate([normal_returns, crash_returns])
np.random.shuffle(market_scenarios)

print(f"Market statistics:")
print(f"  Mean return: {market_scenarios.mean():.2%}")
print(f"  Std dev: {market_scenarios.std():.2%}")
print(f"  5th percentile: {np.percentile(market_scenarios, 5):.2%}")
print(f"  Worst return: {market_scenarios.min():.2%}")


# Compute risk measures for different allocations
allocations = np.linspace(0, 1, 50)

mean_returns = []
var_95 = []
avar_95 = []

for alloc in allocations:
    returns = portfolio_return(alloc, market_scenarios)
    mean_returns.append(returns.mean())
    var_95.append(np.percentile(returns, 5))  # VaR is left tail for losses
    # AVaR = mean of worst 5%
    threshold = np.percentile(returns, 5)
    avar_95.append(returns[returns <= threshold].mean())


fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

# Risk-return tradeoff
ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(allocations, mean_returns, 'b-', linewidth=2, label='Expected Return')
ax.plot(allocations, var_95, 'r--', linewidth=2, label='VaR$_{0.05}$ (5% worst)')
ax.plot(allocations, avar_95, 'orange', linewidth=2, label='AVaR$_{0.05}$')
ax.axhline(0, color='k', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel('Allocation to risky asset')
ax.set_ylabel('Return')
ax.set_title('Risk-Return Tradeoff')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Efficient frontier
ax = axes[1]
# Negative AVaR (we minimize loss, not maximize return)
neg_avar = [-a for a in avar_95]
ax.scatter(neg_avar, mean_returns, c=allocations, cmap='viridis', s=50)
ax.set_xlabel('Risk (negative AVaR)')
ax.set_ylabel('Expected Return')
ax.set_title('Efficient Frontier')
cbar = plt.colorbar(ax.collections[0], ax=ax)
cbar.set_label('Allocation to risky asset')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Risk-Neutral vs Risk-Averse Solutions

from typing import Generic
from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.protocols import Array, Backend
from pyapprox.typing.optimization.minimize.scipy.diffevol import (
    ScipyDifferentialEvolutionOptimizer,
)

# Create objective function classes following pyapprox.typing conventions
class NegMeanReturnObjective(Generic[Array]):
    """Negative mean return objective for risk-neutral optimization."""

    def __init__(self, scenarios: Array, bkd: Backend[Array]):
        self._scenarios = scenarios
        self._bkd = bkd

    def bkd(self) -> Backend[Array]:
        return self._bkd

    def nvars(self) -> int:
        return 1

    def nqoi(self) -> int:
        return 1

    def __call__(self, samples: Array) -> Array:
        # samples shape: (1, nsamples)
        nsamples = samples.shape[1]
        results = self._bkd.zeros((1, nsamples))
        for i in range(nsamples):
            alloc = float(samples[0, i])
            returns = portfolio_return(alloc, self._scenarios)
            results[0, i] = -returns.mean()
        return results


class NegAVaRObjective(Generic[Array]):
    """Negative AVaR objective for risk-averse optimization."""

    def __init__(self, scenarios: Array, beta: float, bkd: Backend[Array]):
        self._scenarios = scenarios
        self._beta = beta
        self._bkd = bkd

    def bkd(self) -> Backend[Array]:
        return self._bkd

    def nvars(self) -> int:
        return 1

    def nqoi(self) -> int:
        return 1

    def __call__(self, samples: Array) -> Array:
        nsamples = samples.shape[1]
        results = self._bkd.zeros((1, nsamples))
        for i in range(nsamples):
            alloc = float(samples[0, i])
            returns = portfolio_return(alloc, self._scenarios)
            threshold = np.percentile(returns, (1 - self._beta) * 100)
            results[0, i] = -returns[returns <= threshold].mean()
        return results


# Risk-neutral optimization using pyapprox.typing optimizer
bounds_arr = bkd.asarray([[0.0, 1.0]])  # Shape (nvars, 2)
obj_neutral = NegMeanReturnObjective(market_scenarios, bkd)

optimizer_neutral = ScipyDifferentialEvolutionOptimizer(
    objective=obj_neutral,
    bounds=bounds_arr,
    maxiter=100,
    seed=42,
)
result_neutral = optimizer_neutral.minimize(bkd.asarray([[0.5]]))
optimal_neutral = float(result_neutral.optima()[0, 0])

# Risk-averse optimization (beta=0.95)
obj_averse = NegAVaRObjective(market_scenarios, beta=0.95, bkd=bkd)

optimizer_averse = ScipyDifferentialEvolutionOptimizer(
    objective=obj_averse,
    bounds=bounds_arr,
    maxiter=100,
    seed=42,
)
result_averse = optimizer_averse.minimize(bkd.asarray([[0.5]]))
optimal_averse = float(result_averse.optima()[0, 0])

print("Optimal Allocations:")
print(f"  Risk-neutral (max expected return): {optimal_neutral:.2%}")
print(f"  Risk-averse (max AVaR_0.95): {optimal_averse:.2%}")

# Compare outcomes
returns_neutral = portfolio_return(optimal_neutral, market_scenarios)
returns_averse = portfolio_return(optimal_averse, market_scenarios)

print(f"\nPerformance Comparison:")
print(f"                      Risk-Neutral    Risk-Averse")
print(f"  Expected return:    {returns_neutral.mean():+.2%}          {returns_averse.mean():+.2%}")
print(f"  Std deviation:      {returns_neutral.std():.2%}           {returns_averse.std():.2%}")
print(f"  Worst 5% avg:       {np.percentile(returns_neutral, 5):.2%}          {np.percentile(returns_averse, 5):.2%}")
print(f"  Worst outcome:      {returns_neutral.min():.2%}          {returns_averse.min():.2%}")


# Distribution comparison
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax.hist(returns_neutral, bins=40, alpha=0.6, density=True, label=f'Risk-neutral ({optimal_neutral:.0%} risky)')
ax.hist(returns_averse, bins=40, alpha=0.6, density=True, label=f'Risk-averse ({optimal_averse:.0%} risky)')
ax.axvline(returns_neutral.mean(), color='blue', linestyle='--', linewidth=2)
ax.axvline(returns_averse.mean(), color='orange', linestyle='--', linewidth=2)
ax.axvline(0, color='k', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel('Portfolio Return')
ax.set_ylabel('Density')
ax.set_title('Return Distribution: Risk-Neutral vs Risk-Averse')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Choosing the Risk Level \(\beta\)
The parameter \(\beta\) controls risk aversion:



\(\beta\)
Interpretation
Use Case




0
Mean (risk-neutral)
No tail concern


0.90
Average of worst 10%
Moderate risk aversion


0.95
Average of worst 5%
Common choice


0.99
Average of worst 1%
High risk aversion


\(\to 1\)
Worst case
Extreme conservatism




# Effect of beta on optimal allocation using pyapprox.typing optimizer
beta_values = [0, 0.5, 0.9, 0.95, 0.99]
optimal_allocations = []

for beta in beta_values:
    if beta == 0:
        # Risk-neutral (mean)
        obj = NegMeanReturnObjective(market_scenarios, bkd)
    else:
        obj = NegAVaRObjective(market_scenarios, beta=beta, bkd=bkd)

    optimizer = ScipyDifferentialEvolutionOptimizer(
        objective=obj,
        bounds=bounds_arr,
        maxiter=100,
        seed=42,
    )
    result = optimizer.minimize(bkd.asarray([[0.5]]))
    optimal_allocations.append(float(result.optima()[0, 0]))

print("Optimal allocation vs risk level:")
print(f"{'Beta':>10} {'Allocation':>15}")
print("-" * 28)
for beta, alloc in zip(beta_values, optimal_allocations):
    print(f"{beta:>10.2f} {alloc:>14.1%}")

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Analytical Risk Measures for Gaussians
For Gaussian distributions, risk measures have closed forms:

# Compare numerical vs analytical for Gaussian case
mu, sigma = 5, 2
analytical = GaussianAnalyticalRiskMeasures(mu, sigma)

# Numerical from samples
samples = np.random.normal(mu, sigma, 100000)

print("Gaussian Risk Measures (mu=5, sigma=2):")
print(f"{'Measure':>20} {'Analytical':>12} {'Numerical':>12}")
print("-" * 48)
print(f"{'Mean':>20} {analytical.mean():>12.4f} {samples.mean():>12.4f}")
print(f"{'Variance':>20} {analytical.variance():>12.4f} {samples.var():>12.4f}")
print(f"{'Mean + 2*Std':>20} {analytical.mean_plus_stddev(2):>12.4f} {samples.mean() + 2*samples.std():>12.4f}")

for beta in [0.9, 0.95, 0.99]:
    avar_analytical = analytical.AVaR(beta)
    threshold = np.percentile(samples, beta*100)
    avar_numerical = samples[samples >= threshold].mean()
    print(f"{'AVaR_' + str(beta):>20} {avar_analytical:>12.4f} {avar_numerical:>12.4f}")

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Entropic Risk Measure
The entropic risk measure provides smooth interpolation:
\[
\rho_\alpha[X] = \frac{1}{\alpha} \log \E_X[e^{\alpha X}]
\]
For Gaussian: \(\rho_\alpha[X] = \mu + \frac{\alpha \sigma^2}{2}\)

alpha_values = np.linspace(0.1, 5, 50)
entropic_values = [analytical.entropic(alpha) for alpha in alpha_values]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.plot(alpha_values, entropic_values, 'b-', linewidth=2)
ax.axhline(analytical.mean(), color='g', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean = {analytical.mean():.1f}')
ax.set_xlabel(r'Risk aversion $\alpha$')
ax.set_ylabel('Entropic risk')
ax.set_title('Entropic Risk Measure (Gaussian)')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
section: When to Use Each Risk Measure
text:
When to Use Each Risk Measure









Risk Measure
Pros
Cons
Best For




Mean
Simple, intuitive
Ignores tail risk
Low-stakes decisions


Mean + Std
Accounts for variability
Symmetric (penalizes upside too)
Moderate risk sensitivity


VaR
Regulatory standard
Ignores severity beyond threshold
Reporting requirements


AVaR
Coherent, tail-focused
Harder to estimate
Financial risk, safety-critical


Entropic
Smooth, differentiable
Less interpretable
Optimization-friendly

title: Optimization Under Uncertainty
text:
Key Takeaways

Risk measures quantify exposure to adverse outcomes beyond expected value
AVaR (CVaR) is the expected value in the worst \((1-\beta)\) outcomes
Higher \(\beta\) = more conservative (focuses on worse scenarios)
Trade-off: Risk aversion reduces expected return but protects against tail events
Gaussian case has closed-form expressions; general case uses Monte Carlo

text:

(Challenge) Implement a multi-period optimization where risk is measured by AVaR of terminal wealth. Compare to single-period AVaR optimization.

text:
TipDownload Notebook

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Identify the physical parameters (moduli, Poisson ratios, geometry) in a composite cantilever beam
Understand the model hierarchy from 2D plane-stress elasticity to 1D Euler-Bernoulli beam theory
Formulate tip deflection as a scalar Quantity of Interest (QoI) for Uncertainty Quantification (UQ)

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Prerequisites
Complete UQ Problem Framing before this tutorial.

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
The Physical Setup
Composite beams with internal holes appear in aerospace, civil, and marine structures. Material properties vary due to manufacturing tolerances, and the question changes from “what is the stress field?” to “how confident are we in the answer?”
This cantilever beam serves as the running example for the UQ workshops. ?@fig-beam-geometry shows the geometry: a rectangular domain with three material layers, five circular holes in the core, clamped at the left edge, and a linearly increasing traction on the top surface.
The beam has three subdomains: stiff skins (top and bottom, \(E_1 > E_2\)) and a compliant core with holes. The four uncertain material parameters – \(E_1\), \(\nu_1\), \(E_2\), \(\nu_2\) – are the inputs we vary throughout the UQ workshops.

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Governing Equations
The stress-strain relationship in each layer follows Hooke’s law:
\[
\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \varepsilon_{kk} \, \delta_{ij} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}
\tag{1}\]
where the Lame parameters \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) depend on the layer’s Young’s modulus \(E_i\) and Poisson ratio \(\nu_i\).
Equilibrium and boundary conditions define the boundary value problem:
\[
\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} = \vc{0} \;\text{in}\; \Omega, \qquad
\vc{u} = \vc{0} \;\text{on}\; \Gamma_D, \qquad
\boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vc{n} = \vc{t} \;\text{on}\; \Gamma_N
\tag{2}\]
where \(\vc{t} = (0,\, -q_0 x/L)^T\) is the linearly increasing traction on the top surface.
The scalar QoI is the vertical displacement at the beam tip:
\[
\delta_{\text{tip}} = u_y(L,\, H/2)
\tag{3}\]

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Reference Solution
?@fig-reference-solution shows the deformed shape and displacement magnitude when the beam is solved at fixed (mean) material properties. The deformation is magnified for visualization; the actual displacements are small relative to the beam dimensions.

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Model Hierarchy: 1D vs 2D
A 1D Euler-Bernoulli beam provides a fast approximation of the tip deflection. The beam equation \(EI\,w''''(x) = q(x)\) governs the transverse (vertical) deflection \(w(x)\) of the beam centerline, where \(q(x)\) is the distributed load per unit length (negative downward). ?@fig-model-hierarchy compares the 1D deflection curve with the full 2D displacement field. Both models predict similar tip deflection, but the 2D model captures stress concentrations around the holes that the 1D model cannot represent.

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Loading the Benchmark
The PyApprox benchmark wraps the full 2D FEM solve into a callable function. The uncertain inputs are Karhunen-Loeve Expansion (KLE) coefficients that parameterize spatially varying Young’s modulus fields over each subdomain.

from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks.instances.pde import cantilever_beam_2d_linear
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks.instances.pde.cantilever_beam import MESH_PATHS

bkd = NumpyBkd()
benchmark = cantilever_beam_2d_linear(bkd, mesh_path=MESH_PATHS[2])
model = benchmark.function()
print(f"Input:  {model.nvars()} KLE coefficients (standard normal)")
print(f"Output: {model.nqoi()} QoIs (tip deflection, total von Mises stress)")

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Key Takeaways

The composite cantilever beam has uncertain material parameters (\(E_1, \nu_1, E_2, \nu_2\)) across three layers
The 2D plane-stress model captures stress concentrations at holes; the 1D beam provides a fast approximation
Tip deflection \(\delta_{\text{tip}}\) is the scalar QoI for all subsequent UQ tutorials
The benchmark API wraps the full FEM solve into a callable function for Monte Carlo sampling

title: Cantilever Beam Model Introduction
text:
Exercises

How would increasing the number of holes affect the tip deflection? Which model (1D or 2D) would better capture this effect?
If the skin stiffness \(E_1\) doubles while the core stiffness \(E_2\) stays fixed, predict qualitatively how \(\delta_{\text{tip}}\) changes and why.

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
TipDownload Notebook





Download as Jupyter Notebook

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Understand how recursion indices define DAG structures
Analyze the impact of DAG topology on variance reduction
Compare ACVMF convergence to MFMC
Determine when different recursion structures are optimal

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Prerequisites
Complete PACV Estimators before this tutorial.

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Recursion Index and DAG Structure
The recursion index \(\boldsymbol{\gamma} = [\gamma_1, \ldots, \gamma_M]^\top\) defines a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where:

Node 0 is the root (HF model)
Edge \((j, \gamma_j)\) indicates model \(j\) is a control variate for model \(\gamma_j\)
The constraint \(0 \leq \gamma_j < j\) ensures acyclicity


DAG Topology Types
Chain (MFMC-like): \(\gamma_j = j - 1\) \[
0 \leftarrow 1 \leftarrow 2 \leftarrow 3 \leftarrow \cdots
\]
Star (ACVMF-like): \(\gamma_j = 0\) for all \(j\) \[
0 \leftarrow 1, \quad 0 \leftarrow 2, \quad 0 \leftarrow 3, \quad \ldots
\]

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Setup

import numpy as np
from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.stats import MultiOutputMean, GMFEstimator, get_estimator

bkd = NumpyBkd()
np.random.seed(42)

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Asymptotic Analysis

MFMC Asymptotic Limit
As the number of LF samples increases (\(N_m \to \infty\) for \(m > 0\)):
\[
\text{Var}(\hat{Q}^{\text{MFMC}}) \to \text{Var}(\hat{Q}_0)(1 - \rho_{01}^2)
\]
MFMC only achieves variance reduction from the first control variate asymptotically.


ACVMF Asymptotic Limit
For ACVMF with all models coupled to HF:
\[
\text{Var}(\hat{Q}^{\text{ACVMF}}) \to \text{Var}(\hat{Q}_0)\left(1 - \mathbf{r}^\top \mathbf{C}^{-1} \mathbf{r}\right)
\]
where \(\mathbf{r}\) is the vector of correlations with HF. ACVMF can achieve variance reduction from all control variates.

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Numerical Demonstration

nmodels = 5

# Covariance with decaying correlations
cov = np.array([[0.9**(abs(i-j)) for j in range(nmodels)] for i in range(nmodels)])
costs = bkd.asarray([10**(4-i) for i in range(nmodels)], dtype=float)

# Correlations with HF
correlations = cov[0, :] / np.sqrt(cov[0, 0] * np.diag(cov))
print("Correlations with HF:")
for i, c in enumerate(correlations):
    print(f"  Model {i}: rho = {c:.4f}")


stat = MultiOutputMean(nqoi=1, bkd=bkd)
stat.set_pilot_quantities(bkd.asarray(cov))

# MFMC (chain structure)
mfmc = get_estimator("mfmc", stat, costs)

# ACVMF (star structure)
acvmf = GMFEstimator(stat, costs, bkd,
    recursion_index=bkd.zeros(nmodels-1, dtype=int))


# Compare at high budget to see asymptotic behavior
budget = 1e7
mfmc.allocate_samples(budget)
acvmf.allocate_samples(budget)

var_mfmc = float(bkd.to_numpy(mfmc.optimized_covariance())[0, 0])
var_acvmf = float(bkd.to_numpy(acvmf.optimized_covariance())[0, 0])

print(f"MFMC variance:  {var_mfmc:.2e}")
print(f"ACVMF variance: {var_acvmf:.2e}")
print(f"ACVMF advantage: {var_mfmc/var_acvmf:.1f}x")

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
When Does DAG Structure Matter?

High Budget Regime
At high budgets, the DAG structure significantly affects achievable variance:

ACVMF achieves the theoretical minimum using all correlations
MFMC limited by first control variate only



Low Budget Regime
At low budgets, both estimators are limited by HF sample count:

DAG structure has less impact
Sample allocation is more important

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Enumeration of Recursion Indices
For \(M\) low-fidelity models, the number of valid recursion indices is:
\[
N_{\text{indices}} = \prod_{j=1}^{M} j = M!
\]
This factorial growth makes exhaustive enumeration expensive for large \(M\).

import math
for M in range(1, 7):
    print(f"M={M} low-fidelity models: {math.factorial(M)} recursion indices")

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways

The recursion index defines a DAG controlling model coupling
Star structures (ACVMF) achieve lower asymptotic variance using all correlations
Chain structures (MFMC) limited by first control variate asymptotically
Optimal structure depends on correlation, cost, and budget
Enumeration is practical for small \(M\) (factorial growth)
At high budgets, DAG structure dominates; at low budgets, sample allocation matters more

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Exercises

(Easy) Verify that the number of valid recursion indices is \(M!\) for \(M\) low-fidelity models by counting for \(M=3\).
(Medium) For a 5-model problem with correlations \(\rho_m = 0.9^m\), find the recursion index that minimizes variance at budget \(10^6\).
(Challenge) Derive the asymptotic variance formula for a general GMF estimator with recursion index \(\boldsymbol{\gamma}\).

title: PACV Recursion Analysis
text:
Next Steps
Continue with:

Pilot Studies - Estimating covariance from pilot samples
Ensemble Selection - Selecting optimal model subsets

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
TipDownload Notebook





Download as Jupyter Notebook

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Explain the key components of an Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) problem
Distinguish between forward and inverse UQ problems
Identify sources of uncertainty in computational models
Frame a scientific question as a UQ problem

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Prerequisites
Complete Setting Up Your Environment before this tutorial.

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
What is Uncertainty Quantification?
Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) is the science of characterizing, propagating, and reducing uncertainty in computational models and their predictions.
In any computational model, we face uncertainties from:

Parameter uncertainty: Model parameters that are not known exactly
Model uncertainty: Simplifications and approximations in the model itself
Numerical uncertainty: Errors from discretization and numerical methods
Data uncertainty: Measurement errors in experimental observations

UQ provides rigorous methods to:

Quantify these uncertainties mathematically
Propagate them through models to predictions
Reduce them through calibration and experimental design

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
The UQ Problem Structure
A typical UQ problem has three main components:

1. The Forward Model \(f\)
A computational model that maps inputs to outputs:
\[
\outputs = f(\params)
\]
where:

\(\params \in \Reals{\dparams}\) are the uncertain parameters
\(\outputs \in \Reals{\doutputs}\) are the model outputs (quantities of interest)



2. The Prior Distribution \(\pdf(\params)\)
A probability distribution describing our uncertainty about the parameters before seeing any data.


3. The Quantity of Interest (QoI)
The specific output(s) from the model that we care about. The QoI is the model output itself: \(f(\params)\).


4. UQ Statistics
Once we have the QoI, we compute statistical quantities to characterize uncertainty in the output, such as:

Mean output: \(\E_{\params}[f(\params)]\)
Output variance: \(\Var_{\params}[f(\params)]\)
Probability of failure: \(P(f(\params) > \text{threshold})\)

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Forward vs Inverse Problems
UQ problems fall into two broad categories:

Forward UQ
Given: Uncertainty in inputs (prior distribution \(\pdf(\params)\))
Find: Uncertainty in outputs
Parameters θ → Model f(θ) → Outputs y
    ↓                          ↓
   Prior               Output Distribution
Examples:

How does parameter uncertainty affect predicted species populations?
What is the probability that system stress exceeds a safety limit?



Inverse UQ (Calibration)
Given: Observed data \(\data_{\text{obs}}\) and prior beliefs \(\pdf(\params)\)
Find: Updated (posterior) distribution of parameters
   Prior p(θ)  +  Data y_obs  →  Posterior p(θ|y_obs)
Examples:

What parameter values best explain observed population data?
How confident are we in the calibrated parameters?

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Running Example: Lotka-Volterra Ecosystem
Throughout these tutorials, we use the Lotka-Volterra model as our running example. This model describes the population dynamics of three competing species in an ecosystem.

from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks import lotka_volterra_3species

bkd = NumpyBkd()
benchmark = lotka_volterra_3species(bkd)

print(f"Model: {benchmark.name()}")
print(f"Number of parameters: {benchmark.nparams()}")
print(f"Number of species (states): {benchmark.nstates()}")


The Model Parameters
The Lotka-Volterra model has 12 uncertain parameters:

3 growth rates \(r_i\): How fast each species reproduces
9 competition coefficients \(a_{ij}\): How species \(j\) affects species \(i\)


gt = benchmark.ground_truth()
print(f"Nominal parameter values: {gt.nominal_parameters}")
print(f"Initial populations: {gt.initial_condition}")



Framing the UQ Problem
For this ecosystem model, we might ask:
Forward UQ questions:

If growth rates are uncertain (say, ±40% of nominal), what is the expected final population of each species?
What is the probability that species 1 goes extinct?

Inverse UQ questions:

Given observed population counts at several times, what are the likely growth rates?
How much does the data reduce our uncertainty about competition coefficients?

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
The UQ Workflow
A typical UQ analysis follows this workflow:

Define the forward model - What does the model compute?
Specify the prior - What do we know about parameters before data?
Choose the QoI - What output matters for our decision?
Propagate uncertainty - Use sampling or surrogates
Analyze results - Compute statistics, sensitivities, or posteriors

The remaining tutorials walk through each step in detail.

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Mathematical Notation
We use consistent notation throughout these tutorials:



Symbol
Meaning




\(\params\)
Parameter vector


\(\dparams\)
Number of parameters


\(f(\params)\)
Forward model


\(N\)
Number of samples


\(\E_{\params}[\cdot]\)
Expected value w.r.t. \(\params\)


\(\Var_{\params}[\cdot]\)
Variance w.r.t. \(\params\)


\(\pdf(\params)\)
Probability density function



See CONVENTIONS.md for the complete notation guide.

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Key Takeaways

UQ systematically handles uncertainty in computational models
Forward UQ: Propagate input uncertainty to output predictions
Inverse UQ: Infer parameters from observed data
A UQ problem requires: a model, a prior, a QoI, and UQ statistics
The QoI is the model output itself; UQ statistics quantify uncertainty in the QoI
The Lotka-Volterra model serves as our running example

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Exercises

(Easy) List three sources of uncertainty in a weather prediction model.
(Medium) For the Lotka-Volterra model, write down a specific forward UQ question and a specific inverse UQ question that a wildlife manager might ask.
(Challenge) Consider a model that predicts bridge stress as a function of traffic load, material properties, and temperature. Identify which parameters might have:

Large uncertainty that significantly impacts predictions
Small uncertainty that can be treated as fixed

How would you prioritize UQ efforts?

title: UQ Problem Framing
text:
Next Steps
Continue with:

Forward Model Definition - Implementing and evaluating the Lotka-Volterra model
Random Variables & Distributions - Specifying parameter uncertainty

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Understand why Sobol indices are random variables under GP uncertainty
Use Monte Carlo sampling to estimate the distribution of \(S_i\)
Recognize the critical requirement: quadrature points \(\neq\) training points
Verify MC convergence rate \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\)

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Understand why Sobol indices are random variables under GP uncertainty
Use Monte Carlo sampling to estimate the distribution of \(S_i\)
Recognize the critical requirement: quadrature points \(\neq\) training points
Verify MC convergence rate \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\)

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Prerequisites
Complete GP Mean with Uncertain Inputs, GP Sobol Indices, and Complete GP Statistics Workflow before this tutorial.

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Conceptual Introduction
When using a GP surrogate for sensitivity analysis, the Sobol indices are random variables because the GP is an approximation with uncertainty. Different realizations from the GP posterior yield different sensitivity indices.

Note: This tutorial assumes a GP with zero prior mean. Extension to non-zero constant mean is future work.

The Monte Carlo (MC) ensemble approach:

Draw random realizations \(f^{(r)}\) from the GP posterior
Compute \(S_i^{(r)}\) for each realization
Use the empirical distribution to quantify uncertainty

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Setup

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Why Sobol Indices are Random
For each GP realization \(f^{(r)} \sim \mathcal{GP}(m^*, s^2 C^*)\): \[
S_i^{(r)} = \frac{V_i^{(r)}}{\gamma_f^{(r)}}
\]
where: - \(\gamma_f^{(r)} = \Var[f^{(r)}]\) is the total variance of realization \(r\) - \(V_i^{(r)} = \Var[\E[f^{(r)} \mid z_i]]\) is the main effect
Since \(f^{(r)}\) varies across realizations, so do the Sobol indices.

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
section: Critical Requirement: Sample Points ≠ Training Points
text:
Critical Requirement: Sample Points ≠ Training Points
At training points \(\vc{x}^{(j)}\), the GP posterior has: - Mean: \(m^*(\vc{x}^{(j)}) = y_j\) (exact interpolation) - Variance: \(C^*(\vc{x}^{(j)}, \vc{x}^{(j)}) = 0\) (no uncertainty)
If we use training points for quadrature, all GP realizations have identical values at those points, giving artificially low variance in \(S_i^{(r)}\).

# Demonstrate zero variance at training points
def rbf_1d(x1, x2, l):
    x1 = np.atleast_1d(x1)
    x2 = np.atleast_1d(x2)
    return np.exp(-0.5 * (x1[:, None] - x2[None, :])**2 / l**2)

N = 5
train_x = np.linspace(-2, 2, N)

A = rbf_1d(train_x, train_x, 1.0) + 1e-10 * np.eye(N)
A_inv = np.linalg.inv(A)

# Posterior variance at training points
K_tt = rbf_1d(train_x, train_x, 1.0)
C_star_train = K_tt - K_tt @ A_inv @ K_tt

print("Posterior variance at TRAINING points:")
print(f"  {np.diag(C_star_train).round(10)}")
print("  (All zeros - no GP uncertainty here!)")

# Posterior variance at quadrature points (different from training)
quad_x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 20)
K_qt = rbf_1d(quad_x, train_x, 1.0)
K_qq = rbf_1d(quad_x, quad_x, 1.0)
C_star_quad = K_qq - K_qt @ A_inv @ K_qt.T

print("\nPosterior variance at QUADRATURE points:")
print(f"  Min:  {np.min(np.diag(C_star_quad)):.4f}")
print(f"  Max:  {np.max(np.diag(C_star_quad)):.4f}")
print("  (Substantial uncertainty - this is what we need!)")

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Monte Carlo Ensemble Implementation

Setup GP and Quadrature

# 1D GP for simplicity
np.random.seed(42)
N = 8
length_scale = 1.0
kernel_var = 1.5

train_x = np.random.randn(N)
train_y = np.sin(train_x) + 0.1 * np.random.randn(N)

A = rbf_1d(train_x, train_x, length_scale) + 1e-8 * np.eye(N)
A_inv = np.linalg.inv(A)

# Gauss-Hermite quadrature (points differ from training!)
Q = 50
quad_x, quad_w = np.polynomial.hermite.hermgauss(Q)
quad_x = quad_x * np.sqrt(2)
quad_w = quad_w / np.sqrt(np.pi)

# GP posterior at quadrature points
K_qt = rbf_1d(quad_x, train_x, length_scale)
K_qq = rbf_1d(quad_x, quad_x, length_scale)

m_star = K_qt @ A_inv @ train_y
C_star = kernel_var * (K_qq - K_qt @ A_inv @ K_qt.T)
C_star += 1e-10 * np.eye(Q)  # Numerical stability

L = np.linalg.cholesky(C_star)

print(f"Training points: {N}")
print(f"Quadrature points: {Q}")
print(f"Posterior mean range: [{m_star.min():.3f}, {m_star.max():.3f}]")
print(f"Posterior std range: [{np.sqrt(np.diag(C_star)).min():.3f}, {np.sqrt(np.diag(C_star)).max():.3f}]")



Sample GP Realizations

n_realizations = 2000

mu_samples = np.zeros(n_realizations)
gamma_samples = np.zeros(n_realizations)

for r in range(n_realizations):
    # Sample GP realization at quadrature points
    z = np.random.randn(Q)
    f_r = m_star + L @ z

    # Compute mean: μ_f = ∫ f(z) ρ(z) dz
    mu_samples[r] = quad_w @ f_r

    # Compute variance: γ_f = ∫ f² dF - μ²
    kappa_r = quad_w @ (f_r ** 2)
    gamma_samples[r] = kappa_r - mu_samples[r]**2

print(f"Sampled {n_realizations} GP realizations")
print(f"\nμ_f samples:")
print(f"  Mean: {mu_samples.mean():.4f}")
print(f"  Std:  {mu_samples.std():.4f}")
print(f"\nγ_f samples:")
print(f"  Mean: {gamma_samples.mean():.4f}")
print(f"  Std:  {gamma_samples.std():.4f}")

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Comparison with Analytical Formulas

# Analytical quantities
tau = quad_w @ K_qt
P = K_qt.T @ np.diag(quad_w) @ K_qt
u = quad_w @ K_qq @ quad_w

eta = tau @ A_inv @ train_y
varpi = tau @ A_inv @ tau
varsigma_sq = u - varpi
zeta = train_y @ A_inv @ P @ A_inv @ train_y
v_sq = 1 - np.trace(P @ A_inv)

E_mu_analytical = eta
Var_mu_analytical = kernel_var * varsigma_sq
E_gamma_analytical = zeta + kernel_var * v_sq - eta**2 - kernel_var * varsigma_sq

print("Comparison: Analytical vs MC")
print("-" * 45)
print(f"{'Quantity':<20} {'Analytical':>10} {'MC':>10}")
print("-" * 45)
print(f"{'E[μ_f]':<20} {E_mu_analytical:>10.4f} {mu_samples.mean():>10.4f}")
print(f"{'Var[μ_f]':<20} {Var_mu_analytical:>10.4f} {mu_samples.var():>10.4f}")
print(f"{'E[γ_f]':<20} {E_gamma_analytical:>10.4f} {gamma_samples.mean():>10.4f}")
print("-" * 45)
print("MC converges to analytical values!")

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Convergence Rate Verification
The MC standard error scales as \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\):

# Compute running estimates
n_points = np.logspace(1, np.log10(n_realizations), 50).astype(int)
n_points = np.unique(n_points)

errors_mu = []
errors_gamma = []

for n in n_points:
    mu_est = mu_samples[:n].mean()
    gamma_est = gamma_samples[:n].mean()
    errors_mu.append(abs(mu_est - E_mu_analytical))
    errors_gamma.append(abs(gamma_est - E_gamma_analytical))

# Fit convergence rate
log_n = np.log(n_points)
log_err_mu = np.log(np.array(errors_mu) + 1e-10)
slope_mu, _ = np.polyfit(log_n[-20:], log_err_mu[-20:], 1)

print(f"Convergence rate for E[μ_f]: n^{slope_mu:.2f}")
print(f"(Expected: n^-0.5)")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.loglog(n_points, errors_mu, 'b.-', label='E[μ_f] error')
ax.loglog(n_points, errors_gamma, 'r.-', label='E[γ_f] error')
ax.loglog(n_points, 0.5 / np.sqrt(n_points), 'k--', label=r'$O(1/\sqrt{n})$')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of realizations')
ax.set_ylabel('Absolute error')
ax.set_title('MC Convergence')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Distribution of Sobol Index (1D Case)
For a 1D GP, the “Sobol index” is trivially 1, but we can examine the distribution of \(\gamma_f\):

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

# Distribution of μ_f
ax = axes[0]
ax.hist(mu_samples, bins=50, density=True, alpha=0.7, label='MC samples')
x_range = np.linspace(mu_samples.min(), mu_samples.max(), 100)
gaussian_pdf = (1 / np.sqrt(2*np.pi*Var_mu_analytical) *
                np.exp(-0.5 * (x_range - E_mu_analytical)**2 / Var_mu_analytical))
ax.plot(x_range, gaussian_pdf, 'r-', linewidth=2, label=f'N(η, s²ς²)')
ax.axvline(E_mu_analytical, color='green', linestyle='--', label=f'η = {E_mu_analytical:.3f}')
ax.set_xlabel('μ_f')
ax.set_ylabel('Density')
ax.set_title('Distribution of GP Mean')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Distribution of γ_f
ax = axes[1]
ax.hist(gamma_samples, bins=50, density=True, alpha=0.7, label='MC samples')
ax.axvline(E_gamma_analytical, color='green', linestyle='--', label=f'E[γ] = {E_gamma_analytical:.3f}')
ax.axvline(gamma_samples.mean(), color='blue', linestyle=':', label=f'MC mean = {gamma_samples.mean():.3f}')
ax.set_xlabel('γ_f')
ax.set_ylabel('Density')
ax.set_title('Distribution of GP Variance')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Correlation Between \(V_i\) and \(\gamma_f\)
The Sobol index \(S_i = V_i / \gamma_f\) is a ratio. When \(V_i\) and \(\gamma_f\) are positively correlated, the ratio has lower variance than expected:

# Correlation between μ_f² and γ_f
# (In 1D, V_i ≈ Var[μ_f] relates to μ_f fluctuations)
mu_sq = mu_samples**2
corr = np.corrcoef(mu_sq, gamma_samples)[0, 1]

print(f"Correlation between μ_f² and γ_f: {corr:.3f}")
print("(Typical values: 0.6-0.8 for well-sampled GPs)")

# Implications for Var[S_i]
print("\nImplication:")
print("  When V_i and γ_f move together,")
print("  their ratio S_i = V_i/γ_f stays relatively stable.")
print("  This reduces Var[S_i] compared to treating them as independent.")

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Practical Guidelines








Parameter
Recommendation
Notes




Quadrature \(Q\)
20-50 per dimension
Total = \(Q^d\) (curse of dimensionality)


Realizations \(R\)
1000-5000
More for accurate tail quantiles


Quadrature location
Different from training
GP has zero variance at training points

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Summary: Two Sources of Error

print("=" * 50)
print("SOURCES OF ERROR IN MC SOBOL ESTIMATES")
print("=" * 50)
print()
print("1. QUADRATURE ERROR")
print("   - Approximating ∫ f dF with Σ w_j f(x_j)")
print("   - Depends on number of quadrature points Q")
print("   - Affects both analytical and MC approaches")
print()
print("2. MC SAMPLING ERROR")
print("   - Finite number of GP realizations R")
print("   - Standard error ~ 1/√R")
print("   - Only affects MC estimates")
print()
print("TOTAL ERROR = f(Q) + O(1/√R)")
print("=" * 50)

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Key Takeaways

Sobol indices are random variables under GP uncertainty
Quadrature points must differ from training points (GP variance = 0 at training)
MC converges as \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\) — use 1000-5000 realizations
\(V_i\) and \(\gamma_f\) are positively correlated (~0.6-0.7), stabilizing \(S_i\)
Report both point estimates and confidence intervals

title: Uncertainty in GP Sobol Indices
text:
Exercises

(Easy) Verify that posterior variance is zero at training points for your GP.
(Medium) Compare Sobol sequence sampling vs random sampling for the quadrature points. Which gives faster convergence?
(Challenge) Compute the correlation between \(V_i\) and \(\gamma_f\) empirically for a 2D GP and compare to the expected ~0.6-0.7 range.

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
TipDownload Notebook





Download as Jupyter Notebook

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Explain why orthonormal PCE bases enable analytical statistics in FunctionTrain
Construct a FunctionTrain with PCE univariate expansions
Use the PCEFunctionTrain wrapper to validate PCE structure
Understand the coefficient matrix representation \(\Theta_k^{(\ell)}\)
Recognize the limitations and requirements for PCE-based FT statistics

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Prerequisites
Complete:

Introduction to FunctionTrain
Polynomial Chaos Expansion

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Why PCE Bases?
Standard FunctionTrain can use any univariate function representation. However, for analytical statistics (mean, variance, Sobol indices), we need special structure.
Polynomial Chaos Expansion (PCE) with orthonormal bases provides exactly this:
\[
\E_{\params}[\psi_\ell(\theta)] = \delta_{\ell 0}, \quad \E_{\params}[\psi_\ell(\theta) \psi_m(\theta)] = \delta_{\ell m}
\]
This orthonormality allows computing expectations by simple coefficient extraction - no numerical integration needed.

From PCE to FunctionTrain
Recall that a univariate PCE represents a function as: \[
g(x) = \sum_{\ell=0}^{p-1} c_\ell \psi_\ell(x)
\]
In a FunctionTrain, each entry of a core matrix is such an expansion: \[
F_{ij}^{(k)}(x_k) = \sum_{\ell=0}^{p-1} \Theta_k^{(\ell)}[i,j] \cdot \psi_\ell(x_k)
\]
where \(\Theta_k^{(\ell)}[i,j]\) is the coefficient of basis term \(\ell\) in the \((i,j)\) entry of core \(k\).

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Setup

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd

bkd = NumpyBkd()
np.random.seed(42)

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Coefficient Matrix Structure

The \(\Theta_k^{(\ell)}\) Matrices
For core \(k\) with ranks \((r_{k-1}, r_k)\) and \(p\) basis terms, we can organize the coefficients into \(p\) matrices:
\[
\Theta_k^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{r_{k-1} \times r_k}, \quad \ell = 0, 1, \ldots, p-1
\]
where \(\Theta_k^{(\ell)}[i,j]\) is the coefficient of \(\psi_\ell\) in \(F_{ij}^{(k)}\).
The core can be written as: \[
\mathcal{F}_k(x_k) = \sum_{\ell=0}^{p-1} \Theta_k^{(\ell)} \cdot \psi_\ell(x_k)
\]
This is a matrix-valued polynomial expansion!


Visual Representation
For a rank-2 core with 3 basis terms:
                        Θ_k^(0)              Θ_k^(1)              Θ_k^(2)
Core k:              (constant)            (linear)           (quadratic)
       ┌────────┐   ┌────────┐           ┌────────┐           ┌────────┐
       │F11 F12│   │θ00 θ01│           │θ10 θ11│           │θ20 θ21│
       │F21 F22│ = │θ02 θ03│ · ψ_0(x) + │θ12 θ13│ · ψ_1(x) + │θ22 θ23│ · ψ_2(x)
       └────────┘   └────────┘           └────────┘           └────────┘

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Creating a PCE FunctionTrain

Using the Factory Function

from pyapprox.typing.probability import UniformMarginal
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.functiontrain import create_pce_functiontrain

# Define marginal distributions for each variable
marginals = [
    UniformMarginal(-1.0, 1.0, bkd),  # x_0 ~ U[-1, 1] → Legendre
    UniformMarginal(-1.0, 1.0, bkd),  # x_1 ~ U[-1, 1] → Legendre
    UniformMarginal(-1.0, 1.0, bkd),  # x_2 ~ U[-1, 1] → Legendre
]

# Create FT with rank-2 interior and degree-3 polynomials
ft = create_pce_functiontrain(
    marginals=marginals,
    max_level=3,      # Polynomial degree per core
    ranks=[2, 2],     # Interior ranks: r_1=2, r_2=2
    bkd=bkd,
    init_scale=0.1,   # Small random initialization
)

print(f"FunctionTrain created:")
print(f"  Variables: {ft.nvars()}")
print(f"  Polynomial degree: 3 (4 terms per univariate expansion)")
print(f"  Ranks: [1, 2, 2, 1] (boundary ranks are always 1)")



Examining Core Structure

# Access individual cores
cores = ft.cores()
print(f"Number of cores: {len(cores)}")

for k, core in enumerate(cores):
    r_left, r_right = core.ranks()
    nterms = core.get_nterms(0, 0)  # All expansions have same nterms
    print(f"\nCore {k}:")
    print(f"  Ranks: ({r_left}, {r_right})")
    print(f"  Terms per expansion: {nterms}")
    print(f"  Number of univariate expansions: {r_left * r_right}")

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
The PCEFunctionTrain Wrapper
The PCEFunctionTrain class wraps a FunctionTrain and validates that all univariate expansions are compatible PCE:

from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.functiontrain import PCEFunctionTrain

# Wrap the FunctionTrain
pce_ft = PCEFunctionTrain(ft)

print(f"PCEFunctionTrain created:")
print(f"  Variables: {pce_ft.nvars()}")
print(f"  QoIs: {pce_ft.nqoi()}")


Validation Checks
PCEFunctionTrain validates:

nqoi = 1: Multi-output not yet supported
Consistent nterms: All expansions in a core have the same number of terms
Has coefficients: All expansions support get_coefficients()


# Example: validation rejects mixed structures
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.functiontrain.core import FunctionTrainCore
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.functiontrain import FunctionTrain
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.affine.univariate import create_bases_1d
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.affine.indices import compute_hyperbolic_indices
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.affine.basis import OrthonormalPolynomialBasis
from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.affine.expansions import BasisExpansion

def create_pce_expansion(degree: int):
    """Create a single univariate PCE."""
    marginals = [UniformMarginal(-1.0, 1.0, bkd)]
    bases_1d = create_bases_1d(marginals, bkd)
    indices = compute_hyperbolic_indices(1, degree, 1.0, bkd)
    basis = OrthonormalPolynomialBasis(bases_1d, bkd, indices)
    return BasisExpansion(basis, bkd, nqoi=1)

# Create two expansions with DIFFERENT degrees
pce_deg2 = create_pce_expansion(degree=2)
pce_deg3 = create_pce_expansion(degree=3)

# Try to mix them in one core (will fail validation)
try:
    # This creates a core with inconsistent nterms
    bad_core = FunctionTrainCore([[pce_deg2], [pce_deg3]], bkd)
    bad_ft = FunctionTrain([bad_core], bkd, nqoi=1)
    pce_bad = PCEFunctionTrain(bad_ft)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Validation rejected: {e}")

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Accessing PCE Cores
The PCE cores provide methods to extract coefficient matrices:

pce_cores = pce_ft.pce_cores()
core0 = pce_cores[0]

print(f"PCE Core 0:")
print(f"  nterms: {core0.nterms()}")
print(f"  ranks: {core0.ranks()}")


Extracting Coefficient Matrices

# Get Θ_k^{(0)} - the constant term coefficients
theta_0 = core0.coefficient_matrix(0)
print(f"\nΘ_0^(0) (constant coefficients):")
print(f"  Shape: {theta_0.shape}")
print(f"  Values:\n{bkd.to_numpy(theta_0)}")

# Get Θ_k^{(1)} - the linear term coefficients
theta_1 = core0.coefficient_matrix(1)
print(f"\nΘ_0^(1) (linear coefficients):")
print(f"  Shape: {theta_1.shape}")
print(f"  Values:\n{bkd.to_numpy(theta_1)}")

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Fitting a FunctionTrain
FunctionTrains can be fitted to data using Alternating Least Squares (ALS):

from pyapprox.typing.surrogates.functiontrain import ALSFitter

# Target function: product with some interactions
def target_function(samples):
    x0, x1, x2 = samples[0], samples[1], samples[2]
    return ((1 + x0) * (1 - 0.5*x1) * (2 + x2) + 0.3*x0*x2).reshape(1, -1)

# Generate training data
n_train = 200
train_samples = bkd.asarray(2 * np.random.rand(3, n_train) - 1)
train_values = bkd.asarray(target_function(train_samples))

# Create fresh FT for fitting
ft_to_fit = create_pce_functiontrain(
    marginals=marginals,
    max_level=3,
    ranks=[2, 2],
    bkd=bkd,
    init_scale=0.1,
)

# Fit using ALS
fitter = ALSFitter(bkd, max_sweeps=30, tol=1e-12)
result = fitter.fit(ft_to_fit, train_samples, train_values)

fitted_ft = result.surrogate()
print(f"Fitting completed:")
print(f"  Sweeps: {result.num_sweeps()}")
print(f"  Final residual: {result.final_residual():.2e}")


Verify Fit Quality

# Test on new samples
n_test = 500
test_samples = bkd.asarray(2 * np.random.rand(3, n_test) - 1)
test_values = target_function(test_samples).flatten()
ft_predictions = bkd.to_numpy(fitted_ft(test_samples)).flatten()

# Compute error
error = np.abs(test_values - ft_predictions)
print(f"Test error:")
print(f"  Max: {np.max(error):.2e}")
print(f"  Mean: {np.mean(error):.2e}")
print(f"  RMSE: {np.sqrt(np.mean(error**2)):.2e}")



Wrap Fitted FT for Statistics

# Wrap the fitted FT
pce_fitted = PCEFunctionTrain(fitted_ft)

print(f"Fitted PCEFunctionTrain ready for statistics:")
print(f"  Variables: {pce_fitted.nvars()}")

# The pce_cores() method provides access to coefficient matrices
pce_cores_fitted = pce_fitted.pce_cores()
print(f"  Core 0 ranks: {pce_cores_fitted[0].ranks()}")
print(f"  Core 0 nterms: {pce_cores_fitted[0].nterms()}")

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Limitations and Requirements

Uniform nterms Requirement
All univariate expansions within a core must have the same number of terms. This excludes “additive” FT structures that mix constant and polynomial expansions:

# This structure is NOT supported for PCE statistics:
#
# Additive FT: f(x,y) ≈ g(x) + h(y)
#
#              [g(x)]   [1  ]
# Core 0:      [    ] · [h(y)]
#              [1   ]   [    ]
#
# This has ConstantExpansion (nterms=1) mixed with PCE (nterms>1)
# in the same core, which fails validation.

print("Additive FT structure is NOT compatible with PCE statistics.")
print("Use multiplicative structures (uniform PCE in all core positions).")



Orthonormality Warning






WarningOrthonormality Required



All formulas for analytical statistics assume orthonormal polynomial bases with:
\[
\E[\psi_\ell] = \delta_{\ell 0}, \quad \E[\psi_\ell \psi_m] = \delta_{\ell m}
\]
Using non-orthonormal bases (e.g., raw monomials) will produce mathematically incorrect results. Always use create_pce_functiontrain with proper marginal distributions.




Supported Polynomial Types



Marginal Distribution
Polynomial Type
Support




UniformMarginal
Legendre
\([a, b]\)


GaussianMarginal
Hermite
\((-\infty, \infty)\)


BetaMarginal
Jacobi
\([0, 1]\)


GammaMarginal
Laguerre
\([0, \infty)\)




from pyapprox.typing.probability import GaussianMarginal

# Mixed marginal types
mixed_marginals = [
    UniformMarginal(-1.0, 1.0, bkd),   # Legendre
    GaussianMarginal(0.0, 1.0, bkd),    # Hermite
    UniformMarginal(0.0, 2.0, bkd),     # Legendre (shifted/scaled)
]

ft_mixed = create_pce_functiontrain(
    marginals=mixed_marginals,
    max_level=3,
    ranks=[2, 2],
    bkd=bkd,
)

print("FT with mixed polynomial types created successfully")
print(f"Each core uses the polynomial type matching its marginal")

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Key Takeaways

PCE bases in FunctionTrain enable analytical statistics computation
Coefficient matrices \(\Theta_k^{(\ell)}\) organize coefficients by basis index
PCEFunctionTrain validates the required structure for statistics
Uniform nterms is required: all expansions in a core must have the same degree
Orthonormality is assumed: use proper marginal distributions for correct bases
Fitting is done with ALS, then wrap with PCEFunctionTrain for statistics

title: FunctionTrain with PCE Bases
text:
Exercises

(Easy) Create a 4-variable FT with different polynomial degrees per variable using the max_level sequence parameter. Verify each core has the expected nterms.
(Medium) Fit a rank-3 FT to the function \(f(x,y,z) = \sin(\pi x) \cos(\pi y) + xyz\). What training sample size gives good accuracy?
(Challenge) Explain mathematically why mixing ConstantExpansion with PCE in the same core breaks the coefficient matrix extraction. What would \(\Theta_k^{(\ell)}\) look like?

text:

FunctionTrain Moments (Math) - Deriving mean and variance formulas
FunctionTrain Moments (Usage) - Computing statistics in practice

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Use the MultiOutputMean, MultiOutputVariance, and MultiOutputMeanAndVariance APIs
Set pilot quantities for estimator covariance computation
Visualize correlation matrices
Work with the statistic protocol hierarchy

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Prerequisites
Complete Multi-Output Statistics before this tutorial.

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Setup

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.stats import (
    MultiOutputMean,
    MultiOutputVariance,
    MultiOutputMeanAndVariance,
    MCEstimator,
)

bkd = NumpyBkd()
np.random.seed(42)

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
The Statistic Protocol Hierarchy
PyApprox uses a three-level protocol hierarchy for statistics:

StatisticProtocol: Basic estimation (sample_estimate, nqoi, nstats)
StatisticWithCovarianceProtocol: + Covariance computation (cov, high_fidelity_estimator_covariance)
StatisticWithDiscrepancyProtocol: + ACV support (get_cv_discrepancy_covariances)

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Working with MultiOutputMean

# Create a 4-output mean statistic
nqoi = 4
stat = MultiOutputMean(nqoi=nqoi, bkd=bkd)

print(f"Statistic type: {type(stat).__name__}")
print(f"Number of QoIs: {stat.nqoi()}")
print(f"Number of statistics: {stat.nstats()}")
print(f"Minimum samples: {stat.min_nsamples()}")


Computing Sample Estimates

# Generate sample values: shape (nqoi, nsamples)
n_samples = 100
values = bkd.asarray(np.random.randn(nqoi, n_samples))

# Compute sample estimate
estimate = stat.sample_estimate(values)
print(f"Sample estimate shape: {estimate.shape}")
print(f"Sample estimate: {bkd.to_numpy(estimate)}")



Setting Pilot Quantities
To compute estimator covariance, we need pilot quantities (the covariance between model outputs).

# Create a covariance matrix from pilot samples
pilot_values = np.random.randn(nqoi, 500)
pilot_cov = np.cov(pilot_values)

print("Pilot covariance matrix:")
print(pilot_cov.round(3))

# Set pilot quantities
stat.set_pilot_quantities(bkd.asarray(pilot_cov))



Computing Estimator Covariance

# Get the high-fidelity estimator covariance for N samples
n_hf_samples = 100
est_cov = stat.high_fidelity_estimator_covariance(n_hf_samples)

print(f"Estimator covariance shape: {est_cov.shape}")
print(f"Estimator covariance (should be pilot_cov / N):")
print(bkd.to_numpy(est_cov).round(6))
print(f"\nVerification (pilot_cov / 100):")
print((pilot_cov / n_hf_samples).round(6))

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Working with MultiOutputVariance

# Create variance statistic for 3 outputs
nqoi = 3
stat_var = MultiOutputVariance(nqoi=nqoi, bkd=bkd)

print(f"Number of variance statistics: {stat_var.nstats()}")
# K(K+1)/2 = 3*4/2 = 6 unique covariance entries


# Compute variance estimate
values = bkd.asarray(np.random.randn(nqoi, 200))
var_estimate = stat_var.sample_estimate(values)

print("Variance estimate (lower triangular):")
print(bkd.to_numpy(var_estimate))


Reconstructing the Covariance Matrix

def reconstruct_cov_matrix(flat_lower, nqoi):
    """Reconstruct symmetric matrix from lower triangular."""
    cov = np.zeros((nqoi, nqoi))
    cov[np.tril_indices(nqoi)] = flat_lower
    cov = cov + cov.T - np.diag(np.diag(cov))
    return cov

cov_matrix = reconstruct_cov_matrix(bkd.to_numpy(var_estimate), nqoi)
print("Reconstructed covariance matrix:")
print(cov_matrix.round(4))

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Working with MultiOutputMeanAndVariance

# Combined statistic
nqoi = 3
stat_combined = MultiOutputMeanAndVariance(nqoi=nqoi, bkd=bkd)

print(f"Total statistics: {stat_combined.nstats()}")
print(f"  - Means: {nqoi}")
print(f"  - Covariance entries: {nqoi * (nqoi + 1) // 2}")


# Generate correlated data
mean_true = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
cov_true = np.array([
    [1.0, 0.5, 0.2],
    [0.5, 2.0, 0.3],
    [0.2, 0.3, 1.5]
])

n_samples = 1000
values = np.random.multivariate_normal(mean_true, cov_true, n_samples).T

# Compute combined estimate
combined_est = stat_combined.sample_estimate(bkd.asarray(values))
combined_np = bkd.to_numpy(combined_est)

print("True mean:", mean_true)
print("Estimated mean:", combined_np[:nqoi].round(3))

print("\nTrue covariance (lower tri):", cov_true[np.tril_indices(nqoi)])
print("Estimated covariance (lower tri):", combined_np[nqoi:].round(3))

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Visualizing Correlation Matrices

def plot_correlation_matrix(cov_matrix, labels=None, ax=None):
    """Plot a correlation matrix as a heatmap."""
    if ax is None:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))

    # Convert to correlation
    std = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov_matrix))
    corr = cov_matrix / np.outer(std, std)

    im = ax.imshow(corr, cmap='RdBu_r', vmin=-1, vmax=1)

    n = len(corr)
    if labels is None:
        labels = [f'{i}' for i in range(n)]

    ax.set_xticks(range(n))
    ax.set_yticks(range(n))
    ax.set_xticklabels(labels)
    ax.set_yticklabels(labels)

    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            ax.text(j, i, f'{corr[i,j]:.2f}', ha='center', va='center',
                   color='white' if abs(corr[i,j]) > 0.5 else 'black')

    plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label='Correlation')
    return ax


# Plot correlation matrix
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

labels = [f'$f_{i+1}$' for i in range(nqoi)]

ax = plot_correlation_matrix(cov_true, labels, axes[0])
ax.set_title('True Correlation')

est_cov = reconstruct_cov_matrix(combined_np[nqoi:], nqoi)
ax = plot_correlation_matrix(est_cov, labels, axes[1])
ax.set_title('Estimated Correlation')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Using Statistics with Estimators

# Create statistic and set pilot quantities
nqoi = 2
stat = MultiOutputMean(nqoi=nqoi, bkd=bkd)

# Define a known covariance structure
pilot_cov = np.array([
    [1.0, 0.8],
    [0.8, 1.2]
])
stat.set_pilot_quantities(bkd.asarray(pilot_cov))

# Create MC estimator
costs = bkd.asarray([1.0])
mc = MCEstimator(stat, costs, bkd)

# Allocate samples
mc.allocate_samples(target_cost=100.0)

print(f"Allocated samples: {bkd.to_numpy(mc.nsamples_per_model())}")


# Get optimized covariance
est_cov = mc.optimized_covariance()
print("Estimator covariance:")
print(bkd.to_numpy(est_cov).round(6))

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Accessing Covariance Components

# The full pilot covariance can be accessed
full_cov = stat.cov()
print("Stored pilot covariance:")
print(bkd.to_numpy(full_cov))

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Key Takeaways

MultiOutputMean: Estimates mean of \(K\) outputs, requires \(K \times K\) pilot covariance
MultiOutputVariance: Estimates covariance, returns \(K(K+1)/2\) values (lower triangular)
MultiOutputMeanAndVariance: Combined estimation, requires additional W and B matrices
set_pilot_quantities(): Sets covariance structure for estimator covariance computation
high_fidelity_estimator_covariance(n): Returns estimator covariance for \(n\) samples

title: Multi-Output Statistics: Usage
text:
Exercises

(Easy) Create a MultiOutputMean for 5 outputs and verify that nstats() equals 5.
(Medium) For a 3-output function, compare the estimator covariance when outputs are independent vs. highly correlated. How does correlation affect the trace of the estimator covariance?
(Challenge) Implement a function that computes the optimal sample size to achieve a target trace or determinant of the estimator covariance.

text:

Multi-Output ACV - ACV for vector-valued statistics
Pilot Studies - Estimating pilot quantities

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
TipDownload Notebook





Download as Jupyter Notebook

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Learning Objectives
After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

Understand why experimental design matters for parameter estimation
Apply D-optimal and A-optimal design criteria
Compute optimal design weights using PyApprox
Interpret optimal designs geometrically

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Prerequisites
Complete Gaussian Process Surrogates before this tutorial.

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Why Design Experiments?
When estimating model parameters from data, not all observations are equally informative.
Example: Fitting a line \(y = a + bx\) from noisy data

Observing at \(x=0.5\) twice: Poor estimate of slope
Observing at \(x=0\) and \(x=1\): Best estimate of slope

Optimal experimental design (OED) chooses where to collect data to minimize parameter uncertainty.

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
The Design Problem

Linear Model Setup
Consider a linear model:
\[
y(\vc{x}) = \boldsymbol{\phi}(\vc{x})^T \boldsymbol{\beta} + \epsilon
\]
where:

\(\boldsymbol{\phi}(\vc{x})\) = basis functions evaluated at design point \(\vc{x}\)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) = parameters to estimate
\(\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\) = observation noise



Information Matrix
The Fisher information matrix is:
\[
M_1(w) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \boldsymbol{\phi}_k \boldsymbol{\phi}_k^T
\]
where \(w_k\) is the “weight” (fraction of observations) at design point \(k\).
The parameter covariance is proportional to \(M_1^{-1}\):
\[
\Cov_\epsilon(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) \propto M_1(w)^{-1}
\]
Goal: Choose weights \(w\) to minimize parameter uncertainty.

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Design Criteria
Different criteria measure “uncertainty” differently:








Criterion
Objective
Interpretation




D-optimal
\(-\log\det(M_1)\)
Volume of confidence ellipsoid


A-optimal
\(\text{trace}(M_1^{-1})\)
Sum of parameter variances


C-optimal
\(\vc{c}^T M_1^{-1} \vc{c}\)
Variance of linear combination


G-optimal
\(\max_k \boldsymbol{\phi}_k^T M_1^{-1} \boldsymbol{\phi}_k\)
Maximum prediction variance




D-Optimal Design
D-optimal minimizes the determinant of the covariance matrix:
\[
\min_w -\log\det(M_1(w))
\]
This is equivalent to minimizing the volume of the parameter confidence ellipsoid.


A-Optimal Design
A-optimal minimizes the trace of the covariance matrix:
\[
\min_w \text{trace}(M_1(w)^{-1})
\]
This minimizes the average parameter variance.

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Setup

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.expdesign.local.design_matrices import LeastSquaresDesignMatrices
from pyapprox.typing.expdesign.local.criteria import DOptimalCriterion, AOptimalCriterion
from pyapprox.typing.expdesign.local.solver import ScipyLocalOEDSolver

bkd = NumpyBkd()
np.random.seed(42)

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Example: Polynomial Regression
Consider fitting a quadratic: \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2\).
The basis functions are \(\phi(x) = [1, x, x^2]\).

# Candidate design points
n_candidates = 21
x_candidates = np.linspace(-1, 1, n_candidates)

# Basis functions: [1, x, x^2]
design_factors = np.column_stack([
    np.ones(n_candidates),
    x_candidates,
    x_candidates**2
])

print(f"Design factors shape: {design_factors.shape}")
print("First few rows:")
print(design_factors[:5].round(3))

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Computing Design Matrices

# Create design matrices object
dm = LeastSquaresDesignMatrices(
    design_factors=bkd.asarray(design_factors),
    bkd=bkd
)

print(f"Number of design points: {dm.ndesign_pts()}")
print(f"Number of design variables (basis terms): {dm.ndesign_vars()}")


# Compute information matrix for uniform design
uniform_weights = bkd.asarray(np.ones((n_candidates, 1)) / n_candidates)
M1_uniform = dm.M1(uniform_weights)

print("Information matrix (uniform weights):")
print(bkd.to_numpy(M1_uniform).round(4))

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
D-Optimal Design

# Create D-optimal criterion
d_criterion = DOptimalCriterion(dm, bkd)

# Evaluate at uniform weights
d_value = d_criterion(uniform_weights)
print(f"D-optimal criterion (uniform): {float(d_value[0,0]):.4f}")


# Find optimal design
d_solver = ScipyLocalOEDSolver(d_criterion, bkd, verbosity=0)
d_optimal_weights = d_solver.construct()

# Extract optimal weights
d_weights_np = bkd.to_numpy(d_optimal_weights).flatten()

print("\nD-optimal design:")
print(f"Total weight: {d_weights_np.sum():.4f}")

# Show non-zero weights
nonzero_idx = np.where(d_weights_np > 0.01)[0]
print("\nOptimal design points:")
for idx in nonzero_idx:
    print(f"  x = {x_candidates[idx]:6.3f}, weight = {d_weights_np[idx]:.4f}")


# Compare criterion values
d_value_optimal = d_criterion(d_optimal_weights)
print(f"\nD-optimal criterion:")
print(f"  Uniform design: {float(d_value[0,0]):.4f}")
print(f"  Optimal design: {float(d_value_optimal[0,0]):.4f}")
print(f"  Improvement: {(float(d_value[0,0]) - float(d_value_optimal[0,0])):.4f}")

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Visualizing the D-Optimal Design

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

# Design weights
ax = axes[0]
ax.bar(x_candidates, d_weights_np, width=0.08, alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel('Design point x')
ax.set_ylabel('Weight')
ax.set_title('D-Optimal Design Weights')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Confidence ellipsoid (projected)
ax = axes[1]

# Compute covariance for both designs
M1_optimal = dm.M1(d_optimal_weights)
cov_uniform = np.linalg.inv(bkd.to_numpy(M1_uniform))
cov_optimal = np.linalg.inv(bkd.to_numpy(M1_optimal))

# Plot 2D projection (beta_1, beta_2)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 100)

# Uniform ellipse
eigvals_u, eigvecs_u = np.linalg.eigh(cov_uniform[1:3, 1:3])
scale_u = np.sqrt(eigvals_u) * 2.5  # Chi-squared scale
ellipse_u = eigvecs_u @ (scale_u[:, None] * np.vstack([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)]))

# Optimal ellipse
eigvals_o, eigvecs_o = np.linalg.eigh(cov_optimal[1:3, 1:3])
scale_o = np.sqrt(eigvals_o) * 2.5
ellipse_o = eigvecs_o @ (scale_o[:, None] * np.vstack([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)]))

ax.plot(ellipse_u[0], ellipse_u[1], 'b-', linewidth=2, label='Uniform')
ax.plot(ellipse_o[0], ellipse_o[1], 'r-', linewidth=2, label='D-optimal')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\beta_1$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\beta_2$')
ax.set_title('95% Confidence Ellipsoids')
ax.legend()
ax.axis('equal')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
A-Optimal Design

# Create A-optimal criterion
a_criterion = AOptimalCriterion(dm, bkd)

# Find optimal design
a_solver = ScipyLocalOEDSolver(a_criterion, bkd, verbosity=0)
a_optimal_weights = a_solver.construct()

a_weights_np = bkd.to_numpy(a_optimal_weights).flatten()

print("A-optimal design:")
nonzero_idx = np.where(a_weights_np > 0.01)[0]
for idx in nonzero_idx:
    print(f"  x = {x_candidates[idx]:6.3f}, weight = {a_weights_np[idx]:.4f}")

title: Optimal Experimental Design
section: Comparing D and A Optimal Designs
text:
Comparing D and A Optimal Designs

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

# D-optimal
ax = axes[0]
ax.bar(x_candidates - 0.04, d_weights_np, width=0.08, alpha=0.7, label='D-optimal')
ax.bar(x_candidates + 0.04, a_weights_np, width=0.08, alpha=0.7, label='A-optimal')
ax.set_xlabel('Design point x')
ax.set_ylabel('Weight')
ax.set_title('D-Optimal vs A-Optimal Designs')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Trace comparison
ax = axes[1]
# Compute trace (A-criterion) for both
M1_a = dm.M1(a_optimal_weights)
trace_uniform = np.trace(np.linalg.inv(bkd.to_numpy(M1_uniform)))
trace_d = np.trace(np.linalg.inv(bkd.to_numpy(M1_optimal)))
trace_a = np.trace(np.linalg.inv(bkd.to_numpy(M1_a)))

bars = ax.bar(['Uniform', 'D-optimal', 'A-optimal'],
              [trace_uniform, trace_d, trace_a], alpha=0.7)
ax.set_ylabel(r'Trace$(M_1^{-1})$')
ax.set_title('A-Criterion (lower is better)')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Trace of covariance:")
print(f"  Uniform:   {trace_uniform:.4f}")
print(f"  D-optimal: {trace_d:.4f}")
print(f"  A-optimal: {trace_a:.4f}")

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Classical Results
For polynomial regression on \([-1, 1]\), optimal designs have known forms:

Degree-1 (Line)
D-optimal and A-optimal both place weight at endpoints: - 50% at \(x = -1\) - 50% at \(x = +1\)


Degree-2 (Quadratic)
D-optimal places weight at three points: - 25% at \(x = -1\) - 50% at \(x = 0\) - 25% at \(x = +1\)

# Verify our result matches theory
print("Theoretical D-optimal for quadratic:")
print("  x = -1.0, weight = 0.25")
print("  x =  0.0, weight = 0.50")
print("  x =  1.0, weight = 0.25")

print("\nOur numerical result:")
for idx in np.where(d_weights_np > 0.01)[0]:
    print(f"  x = {x_candidates[idx]:5.2f}, weight = {d_weights_np[idx]:.4f}")

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Heteroscedastic Noise
When noise varies across design points, the optimal design changes.

# Higher noise at boundary
noise_mult = 1.0 + 0.5 * np.abs(x_candidates)  # More noise at edges

dm_hetero = LeastSquaresDesignMatrices(
    design_factors=bkd.asarray(design_factors),
    bkd=bkd,
    noise_mult=bkd.asarray(noise_mult)
)

d_criterion_hetero = DOptimalCriterion(dm_hetero, bkd)
d_solver_hetero = ScipyLocalOEDSolver(d_criterion_hetero, bkd, verbosity=0)
d_optimal_hetero = d_solver_hetero.construct()

d_weights_hetero = bkd.to_numpy(d_optimal_hetero).flatten()


fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.bar(x_candidates - 0.04, d_weights_np, width=0.08, alpha=0.7, label='Homoscedastic')
ax.bar(x_candidates + 0.04, d_weights_hetero, width=0.08, alpha=0.7, label='Heteroscedastic')
ax.plot(x_candidates, noise_mult/10, 'k--', label='Noise level (scaled)', alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel('Design point x')
ax.set_ylabel('Weight')
ax.set_title('D-Optimal Design: Homoscedastic vs Heteroscedastic')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Heteroscedastic D-optimal design:")
for idx in np.where(d_weights_hetero > 0.01)[0]:
    print(f"  x = {x_candidates[idx]:5.2f}, weight = {d_weights_hetero[idx]:.4f}")

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Practical Considerations

1. Continuous vs Discrete Designs

Continuous designs: Weights \(w_k\) can be any value in \([0,1]\)
Discrete designs: Must allocate integer number of experiments

For small experiments, rounding continuous designs may not be optimal.


2. Model Uncertainty
OED assumes the model form is known. If uncertain:

Use Bayesian model averaging
Design for multiple candidate models
Consider sequential/adaptive designs



3. Computational Cost
For large candidate sets:

Use exchange algorithms
Consider approximations (Fedorov exchange)
Leverage problem structure

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Key Takeaways

Optimal experimental design chooses where to observe to minimize parameter uncertainty
D-optimal minimizes confidence ellipsoid volume (determinant)
A-optimal minimizes average parameter variance (trace)
Design depends on basis functions and noise structure
Classical results exist for polynomial regression on standard intervals

title: Optimal Experimental Design
text:
Exercises

(Easy) Verify that D-optimal design for linear regression (\(y = a + bx\)) places 50% weight at each endpoint.
(Medium) Compare D-optimal and A-optimal designs for cubic regression. Which criterion places more weight at the center?
(Challenge) Design an experiment for estimating parameters of the Michaelis-Menten model \(y = V_{max} x / (K_m + x)\). Hint: Linearize and apply OED, or use nonlinear OED.
